title: BETWEEN DANIEL MORGAN v DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL IN THE MATTER BETWEEN DANIEL MORGAN CLAIMANT AND R P H LIMITED T/A FLORIDA POOLS & SPAS
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT AND
DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 IN
THE MATTER BETWEEN 

 DANIEL
 MORGAN 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 R P H LIMITED 

 T/A FLORIDA POOLS & SPAS 

 RESPONDENTS 

TRIBUNAL JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2025]
TRE 031 

 Date
of Hearing: 6 August 2025 

 Before:
 Mrs
H Westmacott, Deputy Chairman 

 For
the Claimant: In
person 

 For the Respondent: Advocate O
Blakeley, Blakeley Legal 

 DECISION 

 The claim for constructive
unfair dismissal succeeds and the Respondent shall pay £4,712 in compensation
to the Claimant. The claim for unpaid wages fails. 

REASONS 

 Introduction 

 1. The
Claimant was employed by the Respondent from 30 January 2024 to 27 February
2025 as a pool
service engineer. 

 2. The
Claimant issued a claim form with the tribunal on 3 March 2025 claiming constructive
unfair dismissal and unpaid wages against the Respondent. By a response form
presented on 27 March 2025, the Respondent denied the claims. 

 3. A
case management hearing was held on 12 May 2025, at which a final hearing was
listed to determine the claims (the “Hearing”). This judgment records my
decision together with the reasons for it, following that Hearing. 

 4. In
advance of the Hearing I was provided with a file containing the documents the
parties wished to put before me in support of the claim and the response (the
“File”). The numbers in square brackets below are references to the relevant
page numbers of the File. 

 5. At
the outset of the Hearing the Respondent indicated it did not agree to the
documents at pages 132 to 138 being included in the File on the basis they were
not relevant to the claims or response. I agree that they are not relevant and
I did not consider them when reaching my decision on the claims. 

 6. In
advance of the Hearing, I was also provided with written statements from the Claimant
and, on behalf of the Respondent, from Alan Corke (General Manager), Shane
Turrell (Managing Director), Georgie Mason (Office / Accounts Manager) and Emma
Terry (Managing Director), all of whom attended the Hearing and responded to questions
put to them during the Hearing. 

 Unfair Dismissal Claim 

 Evidence 

 7. On
26 January 2024 the Claimant signed a contract with the Respondent stating that
he would have no guaranteed hours of work and would only be paid for the hours he
worked [30-32]. 

 April
2024 

 8. The
parties agree that in April 2024 the Claimant’s contract changed so that he was
contracted to work at least 38 hours per week (the “38 Hours Term”) and that,
although no new contract was signed, this new term was agreed orally and
impliedly by the parties conduct, in that the Claimant continued to work for
the Respondent and the Respondent continued to pay him for that work on the
basis of this verbal agreement. 

 9. Ms
Terry states that during a meeting with the Claimant on 24 April 2024 he was
presented with a new employment contract. A copy of this has not been provided
to the Tribunal, but paragraph 8 of Ms Terry’s statement states: 

 “ This
document reflected the proposed change from a zero-hour arrangement to a full-time
position, outlining the new terms and conditions of [the Claimant’s] employment.
The entirety of the new contract terms, including working hours, remuneration,
and entitlements, were explained to him in detail. ” 

 10. At
the Hearing Ms Terry clarified that while she did meet with the Claimant on
this date, she did not provide the Claimant with a copy of the new contract nor
explain its terms to him. She also does not specifically recall anyone else
doing this, but she said it would have happened as this is what either Ms Mason
or another employee would have done. Ms Mason’s statement does not refer to
doing this nor do any other witnesses’ statements. 

 11. At
the Hearing the Claimant said he did not recall being offered a contract to
sign at this time. He does recall talking to Ms Terry about getting a new
written contract, but does not recall ever receiving one. 

 November
2024 

 12. The
Respondent asserts that, apart from when the Claimant took leave, he always worked
at least 38 hours per week after it was agreed that he would be contracted to do
so, until his employment terminated [21]. 

 13. The
Claimant has not put forward evidence to show that this was not the case, save
that he states in his witness statement that in November 2024 he was only paid
around £900. At the Hearing he mentioned that he took some leave that month. He
did not state whether this was paid or unpaid leave and he did not specify why
his wages did not equate to what he should have been paid had he worked 38
hours per week for the month. 

 14. None
of the Resondent’s witnesses were able to comment on what happened in November
2024 when asked about this at the Hearing and a copy of the pay statement for
that month was not included in the File. 

 January
2025 

 15. The
Claimant asserts that in January 2025 he asked Ms Mason about the amount of his
wages and questioned his holiday pay, as he was concerned because he was being
paid different amounts each month. In his witness statement he said he was
worried because there was little work over the winter, he had witnessed financial
cut backs and he was being told to work faster, so finishing earlier and working
less hours. 

 16. On
27 January 2025 the following WhatsApp message exchange took place between the
Claimant and Ms Mason [84]: 

 From the Claimant: “Please can you send me a copy of my
work contract, thanks” 

 From
Ms Mason: “… The only
contract you’ve ever signed is a zero hour which has been reinstated” 

 From
the Claimant: “ What… oh no, you
can’t just go changing my contracts whenever it suits ” 

 From
Ms Mason: “You never signed
anything so legally you’re on a zero hour. It’s not about suiting us it’s about
what we can offer you and what time off you…” [the remainder of the message
is not included in the File]. 

 17. On
28 January 2025 the Claimant was paid his salary plus 6% for annual leave [129].
It is understood that this was the way he was paid prior to April 2024 when he did
not have any agreed contractual hours. After April 2024, the Claimant was no
longer paid a percentage for annual leave, but was instead paid when he took
annual leave. At the Hearing the Claimant said it was important to him that he
be able to take paid annual leave. 

 18. Ms
Mason explains in paragraph 8 of her statement that during a review of all
staff contracts and the corresponding records in the Respondent’s HR system in
January 2025: 

 “ an
administrative oversight occurred, resulting in the Claimant’s record being
mistakenly reinstated to a zero-hour contract status within the payroll system.
This was an unintended error during a period of significant internal change.
More importantly this also did not change his actual contractual status within
the company. ” 

 19. At
paragraph 11 of her statement Ms Mason goes on to explain that the additional 6%
paid for annual leave on 28 January 2025 was paid in error and this amount was
deducted from the Claimant’s February 2025 payment [130]. 

 20. On
29 January 2025 the Claimant met with Mr Turrell (the “January Meeting”). The
Claimant asserts in his witness statement that Mr Turrell told him that there
are two contracts that run side by side, with fixed hours in the summer and in
the winter everyone is on a zero hours contract. However, the Claimant said
that he spoke to other employees who said this was not true. 

 21. Mr
Turrell’s statement references the January Meeting at paragraphs 13 to 15, stating: 

 “ The conversation then progressed to the Claimant’s
employment contract, as he asserted his belief that he was on a zero-hour
contract. I had a quick review of his personnel file during our meeting, indeed
reflecting only a zero-hour contract, so I immediately got clarification [of]
the situation with Ms Emma Terry who explained that the Claimant had previously
been offered a full-time contract but had explicitly declined to sign it. This
significant point had regrettably been overlooked during the recent management
transition. Despite his decision not to sign, it is important to note that the Respondent
consistently treated the Claimant as a full-time employee, ensuring he received
renumeration and full holiday pay entitlements commensurate with a permanent
position. 

 It was further identified that in January; The
claimant had inadvertently received an additional 6% in his salary. This
payroll discrepancy stemmed from his status as an unsigned full-time employee
being incorrectly entered into the system which inadvertently triggered the
additional payment. I transparently explained this over payment to the
claimant, Clarifying that the adjustment would be made in his subsequent salary
payment. To regularise his employment status, I also provided him with a
contract for his immediate signature and to provide clarification. At the
conclusion of [t]his extensive discussion, the Claimant appeared satisfied with
the explanation and the proposed resolution, and he returned to his duties. 

 Following the meeting on 29 January 2025, the Claimant
departed appearing content and continued his work until 4 February 2025.” 

 22. Mr
Corke stated at paragraph 15 of his witness statement that on 30 January 2025,
the Claimant told him that he did not attend work on 28 and 29 January 2025 as
“ a political protest against his contract and working hours ”. 

 February
2025 

 23. The
File contains various WhatsApp messages between the Claimant and Mr Turrell
sent on or around 7 February 2025, the relevant sections of which state
[114-115]: 

 From
the Claimant: “ After talking with you last week I am still unclear
what contract I am actually on. 

 You said there was two contracts which run side by
side and in the winter everyone is on a zero hour - which I have been told this
is untrue. 

 Please can you send me copies of both contracts along
with how many hours I have actually worked on a weekly basis, since working at
Florida pools. 

 Please can you tell me when my contract changed from
zero hour to fixed full-time contract [verbally] by Emma & Tanya. 

 Please can you tell me when it changed from a fixed
full-time contract and back to a zero hour contract again, and can you tell me
why this is, and why it was done without my knowledge and consent, and please
put this in writing to me.” 

 From
Mr Turrell: “ Firstly, I've had a meeting at your request and
already provided you with all the information you requested. Secondly, you have
your contract, and being given all your payslips. ... I suggest you go and take
advice. When you are back to work I suggest you arrange a meeting with me so I
can again go through your questions.” 

 From the Claimant: “…
Nearly 3 weeks into Jan 2025 I was told I was being put onto a zero hour
contract which was without my knowledge or consent, and I was forced onto it,
being made effective since 1st Jan 2025. 

 I was told after questioning why I wasn't being paid
38 hours, that I had only signed a zero hour contract and it has now been
reinstated, without my knowledge, or consent. … 

 The reason I am asking ‘can you send me copies of both
contracts along with how many hours I have actually worked on a weekly basis,
since working at Florida pools’ is to determine what contract I was on. 

 I have sought advice and have been asked to ask you to
put everything I had asked in my previous message in writing to me please.” 

 24. At
the Hearing the Claimant stated that he recalled Mr Turrell looking through a
file at the January Meeting, but he does not recall being given any document to
sign. He said that he is human and can make mistakes, but he does not think he
is mistaken on this occasion, particularly given that following the January Meeting
he sent a WhatsApp message to Mr Turrell asking for copies of the contracts Mr
Turrell had referenced at the meeting [114]. He said he would not have done
this had he been given a copy of his contract at the meeting. He also does not
recall Mr Turrell explaining the 6% overpayment. 

 25. At
the Hearing Mr Turrell stated that he was sure he gave the Claimant a copy of
the contract at the meeting, referencing a “blank” copy being either printed
off or taken from a file and given to the Claimant. When asked for
clarification on what he meant by a “blank” copy he said that the contract did
include some of the Claimant’s personal details, but he did not seem certain on
exactly which details. Mr Turrell was not able to explain why a copy of the contract
was not included in the File or was not available at the Hearing, save to say
his representatives did not include it. 

 26. At
the Hearing the Claimant directed the tribunal to further WhatsApp messages
that he sent to Mr Turrell [118]: 

 On
13 February 2025 stating: “Hi
Shane 

 Social have asked me to get from you proof that you're
not paying me anything whilst I am off sick, and with my hourly rate and your [contact]
details, please. 

 You can also send us WhatsApp messages just confirming. 

 Thanks” 

 On 14 February 2025 stating: “ Hi Shane, Please can you confirm that you’re not
paying me anything whilst I am off sick, with my salary and your contact
details?” 

 27. The
Claimant noted that Mr Turrell also did not respond to the questions asked in
these messages. At the Hearing Mr Turrell stated that he chose not to reply to
these messages, saying it was a matter for Ms Mason to deal with. 

 28. On
20 February 2025 the Claimant sent an email to the Respondent which, in part,
states [126]: 

 “I
have repeatedly
requested written clarification on why my contract has been changed to a
zero-hour contract without my knowledge or agreement. … 

 … I do not agree to revert to a zero-hour contract, as
has been suggested by yourself and the office. I should be receiving my
contracted 38 hours per weeks consistently throughout the year. 

 Since you have stated that I am to remain on a zero-hour
contract, I will be contesting this decision. Unfortunately, due to these
circumstances, I am left with no choice but to submit my resignation.” 

 29. Paragraphs
18 and 19 of Mr Turrell’s statement state: 

 “ From WhatsApp messages I received from the Claimant,
it was very clear he held the belief that his contract differed from those of
other full-time employees, specifically regarding a winter hour zero-hour
clause. It is my belief that this perception significantly contributed to his
subsequent resignation. 

 Ultimately, the Claimant submitted his resignation on
20 February 2025. ... It subsequently became apparent that the Claimant may
have held a misunderstanding regarding the nature of the full-time contract,
specifically concerning the inclusion of ‘winter hours’, which, by industry
standard, often involve reduced or effectively zero working hours due to
seasonal demand. This misinterpretation was not raised or clarified by the
Claimant during our comprehensive meeting on 29 January 2025, nor prior to his
resignation.” 

 Submissions 

 30. Advocate
Blakeley gave oral submissions at the Hearing. He submitted that it was not
necessary to provide a copy of the contract that the Respondent asserts was
provided to the Claimant on 26 April 2024 and 29 January 2025 for the Hearing.
He further submitted it would be wrong to import all of the terms of that
contract into the terms of the Claimant’s employment contract. The only term
that can be imported is the 38 Hours Term, and that this term was never
breached. 

 31. One
of the issues identified at the case management hearing as set out in the
orders issued following the case management hearing was: “Did the Respondent
unilaterally change the Claimant’s contract from a fixed term contract to a
zero hours contract, or did it represent to the Claimant that it did?” 

 32. When
asked whether Advocate Blakeley thought it would be considered a fundamental
breach of contract if the Respondent were to represent to the Claimant that it
had changed or intended to change his contract back to a zero hours contract,
he said he thought this could be a fundamental breach. 

 33. However,
Advocate Blakeley also submitted that, if the Claimant’s contractual terms
remain as set out in the written contract that was signed by the Claimant, save
that he was now guaranteed a minimum number of hours, then the terms of the
employee handbook are relevant as they are expressly incorporated into this
contract. On page 5 of the Employee Handbook, under the heading “Pay, Etc” at
section D) headed “Shortage of work” it states: 

 “ If there is a temporary shortage of work for any
reason, we will try to maintain your continuity of employment even if this
necessitates placing you on short time or having to lay you off work without
pay." 

 34. Advocate Blakely
submits that this would allow the Respondent to give the Claimant zero
guaranteed hours over winter if necessary. 

 Relevant
Law 

 35. The
recent tribunal judgment in the case of Rasdall v TP Global Finance
Limited [2024] TRE 238 sets out the law on constructive dismissal as it
has been summarised many times in earlier tribunal judgments. In addition, it
makes references to a number of other UK cases which are helpful to consider
when deciding whether there was constructive dismissal in this case. Paragraphs
4 to 9 of the judgment in Rasdall state: 

 “4. The law on constructive unfair dismissal is
summarised in paragraphs 19 to 26 of Dunham v Lightening Hotels Limited [2021]
TRE 55. 

 19. Article 62 EJL sets out the
circumstances in which an employee is dismissed including the following, which
is commonly known as constructive dismissal: 

 “62 Circumstances in which an employee is
dismissed 

 (1) For the purposes of this Part an employee
is dismissed by his or her employer if ... 

 (c) the employee terminates the contract
under which he or she is employed (with or without notice) in circumstances in
which the employee is entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the
employer's conduct.” 

 20. It is well established under case law
that the conditions which must be met to prove constructive dismissal
are: 

 (a) the employer must be in breach of either
an express or an implied contractual term; 

 (b) the
breach must be fundamental, amounting to a repudiatory breach; 

 (c) the employee must resign, at least in
part, in response to the breach; and 

 (d) the
employee must not have waived the breach and affirmed the contract. 

 21. In order for the breach
to be sufficient for the employee to treat the contract as being discharged the
employer’s conduct must amount to a significant breach going to the heart of
the contract or show that the employer no longer intends to be bound by one or
more of the essential terms of the contract. ( Gibb v JAAR [2018]
TRE 135 and Western Excavating (ECC) v Sharpe [1978] ICR 221). 

 22. It is also well established that a term
of ‘mutual trust and confidence’ is to be implied into all contracts of
employment and there is a two-stage test for determining whether there has been
a breach of this implied term ( Maclagan v States Employment Board JET
79/15 and Malik v BCCI [1997] UKHL 23). The Tribunal must be
satisfied that: 

 (a) there
was conduct which destroyed or seriously damaged the trust between employer and
employee; and 

 (b) the employer's conduct was done without
reasonable and proper cause. 

 23. If the employer acts in an unreasonable
way there may be a breach of mutual trust and confidence if the test in Western
Excavating is satisfied, but mere unreasonable behaviour is not
enough. The behaviour must be ‘seriously unreasonable’ or
‘sufficiently serious’ so as to amount to a breach that enables
the employee to treat the contract as being discharged. 

 24. Any
breach of this implied term is a fundamental breach amounting to a repudiation,
which necessarily goes to the root of the contract and allows the employee to
resign and claim constructive dismissal ( Maclagan and Morrow
v Safeway Stores Ltd [2002] IRLR 9, EAT). However, if the employer’s
conduct is not sufficiently serious then there is no breach of the implied
term. 

 25. In
order to establish constructive dismissal, the ‘range of reasonable responses
test’, which is the test usually used to establish unfair dismissal, is not
appropriate ( Luce v Newtel Limited [2018] TRE 147
and Buckland v Bournemouth University Higher Education Corporation [2010]
IRLR 445). The test to be applied is an objective one; whether or
not the employer intended to breach the contract is irrelevant ( Lewis
v Motorworld Garages Ltd [1986] ICR 157). It is well
established that the conduct of the employer must be looked at objectively in
all the circumstances and from the perspective of a reasonable person in the
position of the innocent party ( Maclagan and Malik ). 

 26. The breach of the implied obligation of
trust and confidence may consist of a series of actions by the employer which,
taken cumulatively, amount to a breach of the term, although
each particular incident may not do so in isolation. The
Tribunal in Maclagan , having determined whether or
not each individual alleged act and omission was a breach, then summarised
that the test for assessing the whole course of conduct is as follows: 

 “The
Tribunal noted that the test in such situations is whether, viewed objectively,
(i.e. from the perspective of a reasonable person in the position of the
innocent party) there was some act or omission (or series of acts or omissions)
which was “likely to destroy or seriously damage the relationship of confidence
and trust between employer and employee… so that the relationship is
fundamentally damaged and not able to continue as before.” ” 

 5. The
parties brought to my attention some additional authorities. Mr Flynn referred
me to the English case of Quilter Private Client Advisers Ltd v
Falconer and another [2022] IRLR 227 at 115. The judgment highlights
that in assessing whether there has been a breach, what is significant is the
impact of the employer’s behaviour on the employee rather than what the
employer intended. The impact is assessed objectively. Mr Flynn also referred
me to the English Court of Appeal case of Wandsworth London Borough
Council v D’Silva [1998] IRLR 193 CA. The case says a provision in a
contract that permits unilateral variation by an employer needs to be exercised
reasonably. 

 6. Mr Child
referred me to the House of Lords judgment in Malik v BCCI [1998]
AC 20 which says at page 45D that the implied term of trust and confidence can
be excluded or modified by the parties. This means that the
express terms of a contract take precedence over the
implied term. The same point is made in Reda v Flag Limited a
decision of the Privy Council dated 11 July 2002. In paragraph 45 their
Lordships confirmed that the implied term of trust and confidence must yield to
the express provisions of the contract. 

 7. In London Borough of Waltham
Forest v Omilaju [2004] EWCA Civ 1493 paragraphs 16 to 22 the Court of
Appeal provided helpful guidance on the last straw
doctrine. Although the final straw may be relatively insignificant,
it must not be utterly trivial (paragraph 16). It must contribute something to
the breach although what it adds may be relatively insignificant (paragraph
19). An entirely innocuous act on the part of the employer cannot be a final
straw even if the employee genuinely interprets it as hurtful and destructive
(paragraph 22). If the final straw is entirely innocuous, the claim fails and
there is no need to consider the impact of the earlier acts. This is because
the employee, by failing to resign in relation to the earlier acts, has
affirmed the contract (paragraph 21). 

 8. In Kaur v Leeds Teaching Hospital NHS
Trust [2018] EWCA Civ 978 at paragraph 43 the Court of Appeal
confirmed that if there is a course of conduct that amounts to a breach of the
implied term and the employee continues to work they will not be held
to have affirmed the contract. This means that if there are further acts the
employee can rely on the totality of the conduct to establish a breach of the
implied term. 

 9. Finally,
Mr Child referred to the Court of Appeal decision in Abraham v
Nottingham City Council [2018] EWCA Civ 796 paragraphs 83 to 85 which
is authority for the point that a unilateral variation to a contract of
employment by an employer can be accepted by the employee’s conduct including
continuing to work without objection. Whether the employee’s conduct amounts to
acceptance will depend on the circumstances of each case.” 

 36. It
is also relevant in the current case to consider the requirements of article 3(2)
of the Employment Law, which sets out the particulars that a written statement
of terms of employment must contain. Those particulars include: 

 “(d) the scale or rate of remuneration or the
method of calculating remuneration; … 

 (f) any terms and conditions relating to
hours of work (including any terms and conditions relating to normal working
hours); 

 (g) any terms and conditions relating
to – 

 (i) entitlement to holidays, including
public holidays, and holiday pay (the particulars given being sufficient to
enable the employee’s entitlement, including any entitlement to accrued holiday
pay on the termination of employment, to be precisely calculated), …” 

 37. Finally, I also
considered Article 77F of the Employment Law which allows
the tribunal to reduce an award for unfair dismissal by such amount as it
considers just and equitable if: 

 “(5) The
Tribunal considers that any conduct of the complainant before dismissal (or,
where the dismissal was with notice, before the notice was given) that
contributed directly to the dismissal was such that reduction of the award is
just and equitable. … 

 (10) Any
circumstances that the Tribunal considers would be just and equitable to take
into account.” 

 38. I n Wolfson
v Trident Trust Company Limited [2014] JRC 228, Sir Michael Birt,
then Bailiff of Jersey, referred to Article 77F(10) as “ a sweep-up provision
which enables [the Tribunal] to take into account any other circumstances which
would render it just and equitable to reduce the award .” 

 Conclusion 

 39. To determine
whether the Claimant was constructively dismissed I considered the questions
set out below. To answer each question, I applied the law referenced above to
my findings on the facts of this case. 

 Was there a fundamental
breach of contract? 

 40. It
is evident from the Claimant’s WhatsApp messages to Mr Turrell on or around 7 February
2025 that the Claimant understood the Respondent no longer intended to be bound
by the 38 Hours Term of his employment contract and he requested clarification
on this point. After Mr Turrell responded, reminding the Claimant of their
recent meeting and saying “ you have your contact ”, the Claimant again requested
further clarification and asked for the terms of his contract in writing. Mr
Turrell did not send him anything following this date. 

 41. While
Ms Terry asserts the Claimant would have been given the updated terms of his
contract in April 2024, the Claimant does not recall this and no direct
evidence that he was given a copy in April 2024 was put forward. Mr Turrell
asserts that he gave the Claimant a “blank” copy of his contract in January
2025, which was either printed off or came from a file and he was unclear on
which of the Claimant’s details were included in it. Conversely, the Claimant
denies receiving this. In addition, no copy of any contract referencing the 38 Hours
Term was included in the File. 

 42. On
a balance of probabilities, based on the responses the witnesses gave when
questioned at the Hearing and the contemporaneous documentation in the File
(including the absence of the relevant contract), I prefer the Claimant’s
evidence that he was not provided with the updated terms of his employment in
writing. 

 43. I
note that the Claimant was requesting further information and a copy of his
contract from Mr Turrell in the context of a change in relation to his holiday
pay, which was paid on the basis of a zero-hour arrangement, and a written communication
from Ms Mason stating that he was on a zero-hour contract. While Mr Turrell and
Ms Mason gave evidence that this was an error and Mr Turrell said he told the Claimant
this, the Claimant denies being told this and I have not been shown any
evidence that this error was corrected in writing at any stage prior to the
Claimant’s resignation. Although the Respondent states the error was corrected
in the Claimant’s February pay, there is no assertion the Claimant’s February
pay statement was provided to him prior to his resignation on 20 February 2025. 

 44. It
would not have been difficult for Mr Turrell to write to the Claimant between 7
and 20 February 2025 to reassure him that he would be offered 38 hours per week
going forward, but he did not do this. In addition, Mr Turrell chose not to
respond to the Claimant’s requests on 13 and 14 February 2025 for information
the Claimant said he needed for Social Security. Mr Turrell gave evidence that
this was a question that should have been put to Ms Mason, yet he did not say
that he told the Claimant this nor that he referred the matter to Ms Mason. It
is understandable that the Claimant felt that the Respondent no longer wished
to engage with him in relation to his employment contract and reached the
conclusion that it no longer agreed to be bound by the 38 Hours Term. 

 45. It
is not clear from the evidence in the File whether it is the Respondent’s
position that the Claimant’s contract included a clause which allowed it to
offer the Claimant less than 38 hours over the winter (a “Winter Hours
Clause”). Both the Response Form and paragraph 6 of Mr Turrell’s statement simply
states that the Claimant “was contracted to work 38 hours per week ”
[19]. While paragraph 18 of Mr Turrell’s statement refers to the Claimant’s “ belief
… that his contract differed from those of other full-time employees,
specifically regarding a winter hour zero-hour clause” and paragraph 19
references the Claimant’s “misunderstanding
regarding the nature of the full-time contract, specifically concerning the
inclusion of ‘winter hours’” , Mr
Turrell does not specify whether it is the Respondent’s position that the
Winter Hours Clause applied to the Claimant or not. However, when asked about
this at the Hearing, Mr Turrell said that all of the Respondent’s employment
contracts, including the Claimant’s, included the Winter Hours Clause. 

 46. The
parties both assert that the Claimant was “ contracted to work 38 hours per
week ”. By this, I understand them to mean that the Respondent agreed to
offer the Claimant 38 hours per week and the Claimant agreed to work that
number of hours. I agree with Advocate Blakely’s submission that the only term
that should be imported into the original signed contract of employment is the
38 Hours Term. Accordingly, I find that there was no Winter Hours Clause agreed
between the parties. 

 47. I
further find that the conduct of the Respondent in January and February 2025,
both in the errors that were made and its failure to respond to the Claimant’s
request for clarification prior to 20 February 2025, represented to the
Claimant that the Respondent no longer agreed to be bound by the 38 Hours Term,
at least not without the Winter Hours Clause also applying. 

 48. I
considered Advocate Blakely’s submission that the Respondent was entitled to offer
the Claimant less than 38 hours per week in accordance with the Shortage of
Work provision in the handbook. However, the first time this was raised was at
the end of the Hearing. The Claimant did not have the opportunity to respond to
this and no evidence was put forward from either party in relation to it’s
applicability. For example, no evidence was given that this was the version of
the handbook that was incorporated into the contract between the parties or whether
a copy was provided to the Claimant at any stage during his employment. I am
also not persuaded that the Shortage of Work provision would be sufficient to
cover the same situation as the Winter Hours Clause. 

 49. Article
3(2) of the Employment Law requires the Respondent to set out in writing,
amongst other things, any terms relating to the Claimant’s hours of work and
entitlement to holidays and holiday pay. Not only have I found that the
Respondent did not do this, but I have found that it failed to do so even after
the Claimant requested clarification of the terms of his employment in writing.
In addition, I have found that the Respondent represented to the Claimant that
it no longer intended to be bound by the 38 Hours Term, thus changing an
essential term of the contract. It was clear the Claimant did not agree to this
and this would, therefore, be a unilateral change to a fundamental term of the contract.

 50. Following
the judgments in the cases of Gibb and Western
Excavating , if the employer’s conduct shows that the employer no longer
intends to be bound by one or more of the essential terms of the contract, this
would constitute a fundamental breach of contract. I have found that the
Respondent’s conduct showed that it no longer intended to be bound by the term
of the contract in relation to the Claimant’s hours of work, which is an
essential term, and accordingly this is a fundamental breach of contract. 

 51. I
find that the Respondent’s conduct seriously damaged the trust between the
parties, amounting to a breach of the term of mutual trust and confidence which
is implied into all employment relationships. In accordance with the cases of Maclagan and Malik ,
I reached this conclusion after considering the Respondent’s actions (and
inactions) from the perspective of a reasonable person in the position of the
Claimant. I have not made a finding as to whether the Respondent intended to
make this representation, but I note that, in accordance with the cases of Lewis 
and Quilter , this is not a significant consideration. 

 52. As
set out in the cases of Malik and Marrow , a breach
of the term of mutual trust and confidence. without any reasonable or proper
cause, is a fundamental breach of contract, which allows the employee to resign
and claim constructive dismissal. Consequently, the Respondent’s conduct
constituted a fundamental breach of contract. 

 Did
the Claimant resign as a result? 

 53. Having
found the Respondent’s conduct constituted a fundamental breach of contract, I went
on to consider whether the Claimant resigned as a result of that conduct. 

 54. The
Respondent has not provided an alternative reason for the Claimant’s
resignation, and I accept that the Claimant resigned because he understood that
the Respondent had unilaterally changed the terms of his contract relating to
hours. This is made clear in the email the Claimant sent notifying the Respondent
of his resignation on 20 February 2025 as well as the Claimant’s witness
evidence. 

 55. Consequently,
I find that the Claimant resigned as a result of the Respondent’s conduct which
constituted a fundamental breach of contract. 

 Did
the Claimant affirm the contract? 

 56. When
determining whether the Claimant did anything to demonstrate that he affirmed
the employment contract following the Respondent’s breach, I considered the
case of Abraham , which made the point that a unilateral variation
to a contract can be accepted by an employee continuing to work without
objection, but whether the employee’s conduct amounts to acceptance will depend
on the circumstances of each case. 

 57. In the current case,
although the Claimant continued to work for a short period, he did not do so
without objection, writing to both Ms Mason and Mr Turrell to object and, the
Respondent asserts, failing to attend work for two days in protest in relation
to his contract and working hours. 

 58. There
is no assertion by the Respondent that the Claimant did anything else to affirm
the breach, he resigned relatively soon after he understood the Respondent
intended not to comply with the 38 Hours Term and shortly after he had requested
clarification and the Respondent failed to provided it. 

 59. Accordingly,
I find that the Claimant did not affirm the fundamental breach of contract
which he resigned as a result of and, consequently, the Claimant was
constructively dismissed. 

 Was the dismissal fair? 

 60. No
fair reason has been given for the dismissal. Accordingly, I find that the
dismissal was unfair and the claim for unfair dismissal succeeds. 

 How
much compensation should the Claimant be awarded? 

 61. As the Claimant had continuous employment of between 1 and 2
years, under article 77F of the Employment Law and the Schedule to the
Employment (Awards) (Jersey) Order 2009, the maximum amount of compensation the
Tribunal can award is 8 weeks’ pay. 

 62. I
have carefully considered whether there is any reason to reduce the award under
Article 77F and, in particular, I note that Mr Turrell suggested in his WhatsApp message on 7
February 2025 that the Claimant arrange a meeting with him on the Claimant’s
return to work to discuss the Claimant’s questions. I considered whether compensation
should be reduced on the basis that the Claimant did not do this prior to
resigning. 

 63. The Claimant
provided contemporaneous written evidence that he asked for his contract in
writing in a situation where the Respondent admits Ms Mason made errors in her
representation to the Claimant of an essential term of that contract and the
Claimant made it clear he was confused by the subsequent discussions he had
with Mr Turrell regarding this. The Respondent provided no persuasive evidence
that the Claimant’s request for clarification was properly dealt with. The
Tribunal was even not provided with a copy of the contract the Respondent’s
witnesses sought to rely on. I am not satisfied that the situation would have
been any different had the Claimant met with Mr Turrell again. Accordingly, I
do not believe that a reduction should be applied on the basis the Claimant did
not wait to meet with him. 

 64. I note that the
Respondent made assertions in relation to the Claimant failing to attend work. However,
I do not consider there to be evidence of sufficiently blameworthy conduct by
the Claimant to make it just and equitable for a reduction in the award on this
basis. Further, I do not consider there to be any other reason which would make
a reduction just and equitable. 

 65. The
Claimant calculates his weekly wage in the claim form as £589, based on £15.50
per hour for 38 hours per week. The Respondent does not dispute this
calculation. Accordingly, 8 weeks’ wages equals £4,712 and the Claimant is
awarded this amount as compensation for unfair dismissal. 

 Unpaid
Wages Claim 

 66. The
basis for the unpaid wages claim is unclear. 

 67. In
the claim form the Claimant calculated this claim on the grounds that on 28
January 2025 he should have been paid 4 week’s wages, which, based on 38 hours
per week at a rate of £15.50 per hour, amounts to £2,556, but he was only paid
£1,946.96. Accordingly, he claimed the difference of £409.04. 

 68. However,
while there is evidence that there was an agreement the Claimant was contracted
to work at least 38 hours work per week, there is no evidence that he should be
paid for 38 hours per week in situations where he was offered this number of
hours but declined to work them. 

 69. In
response to this ground the Respondent asserted that the Claimant was not paid
for working on 21, 22, 27 or 28 January 2025 as he did not work on these dates
and he was absent from work without authorization. At the Hearing, the Claimant
was unclear whether or not he worked on these dates. He thought he may have
been too ill to work on 28 and 29 January 2025, but stated that he did not
notify the Respondent that he was sick on these dates. 

 70. At
the Hearing, the grounds for the Claimant’s unpaid wages claim appeared to be
that, while he was only paid for working 45.5 hours in February 2025, he
thought he had worked closer to 65 hours in February 2025. 

 71. Ms
Mason explained at the Hearing that she calculated that the Claimant worked
45.5 hours in Feburary 2025 based on the times sheets that the Claimant had
submitted. The Claimant did not assert that he had submitted timesheets saying
he worked more hours than this, but he did say his diary may show this. Neither
he nor the Respondent seemed to know exactly where his diary was, but there was
a suggestion that it may be in the Respondent’s yard. The Claimant said he had
asked for it but it was not provided. This was not raised with the Tribunal in
advance of the Hearing and, accordingly, no specific order was made for its
production. 

 72. No
other evidence was provided to show that the Claimant worked for more hours
than he was paid for. Neither was any evidence put forward to show that he
should be paid for any hours, despite not working them. Accordingly, the claim
for unpaid wages fails. 

 Mrs H Westmacott, Deputy Chairman Sent to the
parties on: 3 September 2025 

 Page Last Updated: 21 Oct 2025
tribunal_members: Mrs H Westmacott, Deputy Chairman For the Claimant: In person For the Respondent: Advocate O Blakeley, Blakeley Legal DECISION The claim for constructive unfair dismissal succeeds and the Respondent shall pay £4,712 in compensation to the Claimant. The claim for unpaid wages fails. REASONS Introduction 1. The Claimant was employed by the Respondent from 30 January 2024 to 27 February 2025 as a pool service engineer. 2. The Claimant issued a claim form with the tribunal on 3 March 2025 claiming constructive unfair dismissal and unpaid wages against the Respondent. By a response form presented on 27 March 2025, the Respondent denied the claims. 3.
